gothic.scholarly_consensus: High-confidence reconstruction via Wulfila Codex Argenteus + Greek/Latin transcriptions + PGmc comparisons (Braune/Wright/Streitberg consensus); minor disputes flagged on diphthong monophthongization timing and nasalization extent.
gothic.script_notes: Wulfila's 27-letter Greek-uncial base (4th c.) with runic/Latin additions for unique PGmc sounds (e.g. =þ/θ, =q/kʷ, =ƕ/hʷ or [ʍ]); direct 1:1 mapping to phonemes enables precise video captioning (no silent letters; consistent orthography).

vowels: Short: /a ɛ i ɔ u/; Long: /aː eː iː oː uː ɛː ɔː/; Diphthongs: /ai au iu/ (allophones [ɛ ɔ] before r/h per PGmc).
consonants: Stops p b t d k g kʷ gʷ (with fricative allophones β ð ɣ); Fricatives f θ s z x h hʷ; Nasals m n ŋ; Liquids l r; Glides j w.
phoneme_inventory.proto_germanic_correspondences: Strong boosts: Gothic preserves PGmc /z/ without rhotacism (unlike ON/OE), Verner-law alternations visible, Grimm stops intact; comparative to ON/OE confirms lengths and diphthongs.
phoneme_inventory.distinctive_features: Geminates common; voiced fricatives positional (intervocalic); no umlaut (East Germanic conservatism); nasal longs before h.

ipa_reconstructed.sample_text: Atta unsar þu in himinam, weihnai namo þein
ipa_reconstructed.transcription: /ˈatːa ˈunsar θuː in ˈhiminaːm ˈwiːhnai ˈnaːmoː θeiːn/
ipa_reconstructed.confidence_note: Braune/Wright standard (PGmc vowel correspondences + orthography); minor nasal/quality variants possible but core segments undisputed.

gothic.stress_rules: PGmc root-initial primary stress preserved (fixed, no shift on inflection); secondary on first element of compounds.
gothic.grok_imagine_guidance: English speakers err on dental θ/ð (not stops), fricatives β/ɣ/x (intervocalic), long vowels sustained (macrons), ƕ as [ʍ] or aspirated hw, no schwa reduction, Greek-like vowel qualities; slow pacing with geminates held; laryngeals as [x/h] uvular/fricative.
tutoring_hooks: Sole complete East Germanic corpus (Wulfila Bible translation ~350 AD) — 'missing link' for PGmc reconstruction.
Wulfila invented unique script specifically for Gothic Bible (Arian Christianity among Goths).
'Atta' cognate to English 'dad' via baby-talk; no silent letters ever; conservative phonology retains PGmc z and stops.
Comparative goldmine: Gothic vs ON/OE reveals East vs North/West branches.
Used for converting Germanic tribes — earliest literary Germanic text.
sources: Braune's Gothic Grammar (with Balg); Joseph Wright Grammar; Wulfila Project (wulfila.be); Wikipedia phonology (citing Streitberg, Lehmann); UT Austin EIEOL; scholarly syllabi.

old-norse.scholarly_consensus: Strong from Codex Regius + runes + Gordon/First Grammatical Treatise; West Norse (Icelandic literary) preferred baseline; disputes on nasal vowel persistence timing and ʀ quality.

dialect_notes.west_norse: Iceland/Norway/Atlantic: preserves diphthongs/umlauts longer; assimilation (mp>pp); basis for literary Edda/sagas.
dialect_notes.east_norse: Sweden/Denmark: early monophthongization (ei>ē, au>ø̄ from 10th c.); runic inscriptions more conservative.

vowels: Short/long a æ e i o ø u y ɑ ɔ; early nasals; diphthongs ei au ey/øy.
consonants: p b t d k g; f θ s ʀ h; m n; l r; j w (gemination).
phoneme_inventory.thorn_eth_distinction: þ = voiceless /θ/ (initial, after voiceless); ð = voiced /ð/ (intervocalic, after voiced); MSS use interchangeably but positional allophones (no phonemic contrast).
phoneme_inventory.distinctive_features: Heavy i-/u-/a-umlaut + breaking; no silent letters; geminates; Younger Futhark runic (16 letters, no length distinction).

ipa_reconstructed.sample_text: Hljóðs bið ek allar, helgar kindir
ipa_reconstructed.confidence_note: Gordon/Crawford West Norse standard (PGmc *hl + diphthongs); runic vs manuscript minor shifts noted.

old-norse.stress_rules: PGmc root-initial primary + secondary in compounds (preserved in Icelandic).

prosody.alliterative_meter_note: Skaldic/Eddic: initial consonants (or vowels) alliterate on lifts; key to reconstruction.

old-norse.grok_imagine_guidance: English errs on rounded ø/ǫ (front), ʀ (palatal r-like), long vowels/macrons held, umlauts distinct from modern, no aspiration; voiced ð vs voiceless þ by position; runic period conservative (less umlaut).
tutoring_hooks: No silent letters in any period — spelling phonetic.
Runic (Younger Futhark) vs manuscript Latin shift reveals evolution (e.g. nasal loss).
West/East split directly explains modern Scandinavian divergence.
Völuspá opening summons 'holy kindreds' in alliterative power; preserves PGmc longer than OE.
Umlauts and breaking create 'musical' vowel changes unique in Germanic.
sources: Gordon 'Introduction to Old Norse'; Jackson Crawford reconstructions; Wikipedia (citing First Grammatical Treatise, Codex Regius); Poetic Edda editions.

old-english.scholarly_consensus: Mitchell & Robinson (West Saxon default); strong dialectal evidence; disputes on short diphthong realization (true diphthongs vs length) and palatalization timing (Hogg/Campbell).

dialect_notes.west_saxon: Literary dominant (early: breaking heavy; late: smoothing/vowel shifts); Beowulf mostly late WS.
dialect_notes.northumbrian: Anglian: retains /io/, less breaking, vowel raising/smoothing; Mercian second fronting.

vowels: Short/long: i y u e o æ ɑ; diphthongs eo æa io ie (breaking products).
consonants: p b t d tʃ dʒ k g; f v θ ð s z ʃ ç x ɣ h; m n ŋ; l r; j w ʍ.
phoneme_inventory.thorn_eth_distinction: þ/ð interchangeable spelling for /θ ð/; [θ] voiceless (initial/final/after voiceless); [ð] voiced (intervocalic/medial after voiced sounds).
phoneme_inventory.distinctive_features: Breaking (æ > ea before r/l/h); palatalization (c>g > tʃ/dʒ); geminates; no modern silent letters.

ipa_reconstructed.confidence_note: Mitchell/Robinson + British Library readings standard (WS); PGmc alliteration/stress confirms; Northumbrian variants noted.

old-english.stress_rules: PGmc root-initial primary preserved; unstressed prefixes (ġe-, be-); compounds stress first root.

prosody.alliterative_meter_note: 4 stresses/line (two lifts per half-line); alliteration on first lift (often second); vowels cluster; wr/wl with w.

old-english.grok_imagine_guidance: English speakers: æ as [æ] (cat); hw/ʍ distinct; breaking diphthongs sustained (ea/eo); þ/ð voicing by environment; palatals tʃ/dʒ; long vowels/macrons; alliterative pacing with strong stresses; no schwa reduction.
tutoring_hooks: Alliterative meter like ancient rap battles — rules drive word choice.
Thorn/eth spelling flexible but sound rule strict (positional voicing).
Dialectal vowel changes (breaking/smoothing) explain modern irregularities.
Closest ancestor to English yet alien-sounding; Beowulf sets heroic tone with 'Gardena'.
sources: Mitchell & Robinson 'Guide to Old English'; Wikipedia phonology (citing Hogg 1992, Campbell 1959, Fulk); British Library/OCW readings; Bosworth-Toller; university syllabi.
